sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Marine envenomation can result from stings due to superficial contact or penetrating injuries from spiny marine creatures.
- Stings:
  - Box Jellyfish
  - Irukandji
  - Blue bottle and other jellyfish
  - Blue Ringed Octopus
- Penetrating injuries:
  - Venomous fish
  - Stingray
  - Sea Urchin

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Box Jellyfish
- Severe Localised Pain
- Adherent tentacles and associated lesions
- Cardiovascular collapse can occur within 20 – 30 minutes which can be life- threating if ACLS is not initiated
- Irukandji
- The sting initially appears minor. Approximately 30 minutes following a sting, symptoms appear (generalised pain, agitation, vomiting and diaphoresis)
- The sting is associated with the release of endogenous catecholamines leading to sympathomimetic toxicity where symptoms could include:
  - Hypertension, tachycardia, arrythmias and chest pain. Severe cases may cause MI
  - Agitation, anxiety, seizures, hyperreflexia
  - Dilated pupils
  - Hyperthermia, sweating
  - Goosebumps, rhabdomyolysis
  - ECG changes can occur which can reflect myocardial injury
  - Clinical features of dysphoria, severe generalised pain, sweating, hypotension and pulmonary oedema in the absence of major dermal findings is pathognomonic of Irukandji syndrome
  - Blue Bottle and other minor jellyfish
  - Immediate localised pain that can last several hours with associated linear eruptions
  - Mild systemic presentations with nausea, vomiting and malaise
  - Blue-ringed Octopus
  - Saliva contains tetrodotoxin which is injected with bite

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Most blue-ringed octopus bites occur when the animal is handled
- Do not use pressure immobilisation for jellyfish stings or fish-spine injuries
- Use pressure immobilisation for suspected blue-ringed octopus or cone shell envenomation where feasible
- Treat sea snake bites as for Australian snake bite with pressure bandaging and immobilisation
- Vinegar may help inactivate non-discharged nematocysts in suspected box jellyfish or Irukandji stings, but should not be used for bluebottle stings
- Patients who initially appear stable but develop severe symptoms within 30 minutes may have Irukandji syndrome and require urgent medical attention
- Hot-water immersion is primarily for venomous fish, stingray, or urchin injuries rather than jellyfish stings

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Remove the patient from the water and keep them at rest
- Watch closely for respiratory failure or cardiac arrest; start CPR if required
- Assist ventilations as required
- Observe closely for signs of anaphylaxis; if identified, treat accordingly
- Conduct Vital Sign Survey
- Suspected tropical box jellyfish or Irukandji sting:
  - If the patient is unwell, has more than a small localised sting, or the species is uncertain, call for urgent transport
  - Douse the sting area with household vinegar for 30 seconds if available
  - Remove remaining tentacles carefully and rinse with seawater, not fresh water
  - Apply a cold pack in a dry bag for analgesia
- Clearly localised bluebottle or other minor jellyfish sting:
  - Do not apply vinegar
  - Remove visible tentacles, rinse with seawater, and immerse the area in hot water that the patient can tolerate for up to 20 to 30 minutes
  - If heat is unavailable or ineffective, apply a cold pack
- Venomous fish or stingray injury:
  - Treat major bleeding or any impaled object first
  - If the sting is to a limb, immerse the affected area in hot water that the patient can tolerate
  - Do not use pressure immobilisation for fish-spine injuries
- Suspected blue-ringed octopus or cone shell envenomation:
  - Apply pressure immobilisation if possible
  - Be prepared to support breathing early and continue resuscitation until definitive care

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitoring
- Consider advanced airway management where required
- Gain IV access
- Consider blood draw and troponin if indicated
- Provide analgesia according to severity, local protocol, and broader pain-management guidance rather than a fixed score-to-drug ladder
- Consider antiemetic if required
- Escalate early for respiratory compromise, shock, or severe systemic features

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Caring for the patients in the hours following envenomation (e.g. including inter-hospital transfer care) will include monitoring for signs of coagulopathy (bleeding from bite site or cannula) and renal impairment. This may include measuring urine output where possible
- Pain relief works best with a multimodal approach
